Description: De-identified health records from ICU patients including labs, vitals, and meds.
Multi-center ICU database covering over 200,000 admissions across the US.
Global health statistics from 194 countries tracking mortality, diseases, and nutrition.
Detailed mortality and population data for developed nations globally.
Wide-ranging online data for epidemiological research including natality and mortality.
US FDA data on adverse drug events, labeling, and recalls.
Various lightweight tabular datasets for disease prediction (e.g., breast cancer, heart disease).
Community-curated clean tabular datasets for various diseases and health metrics.
Official US government healthcare dataset repository covering demographics and disease tracking.

Normal vital signs for adults: Heart rate 60-100 bpm (resting), Blood pressure less than 120/80 mmHg, Respiratory rate 12-20 breaths per minute, Oxygen saturation (SpO2) 95-100%, Body temperature 97-99°F (36.1-37.2°C). Children and infants have different normal ranges.

Blood pressure categories: Normal (less than 120/80 mmHg), Elevated (120-129/less than 80 mmHg), Hypertension Stage 1 (130-139 or 80-89 mmHg), Hypertension Stage 2 (140 or higher or 90 or higher mmHg), Hypertensive Crisis (higher than 180/120 mmHg - seek immediate medical attention). Risk factors include age, family history, obesity, sedentary lifestyle, smoking, and high sodium diet.

Normal resting heart rate for adults is 60-100 beats per minute. Athletes may have 40-60 bpm. Tachycardia is defined as more than 100 bpm at rest and can be caused by fever, anxiety, medications, or heart conditions. Bradycardia is less than 60 bpm and may be normal in athletes or indicate heart block. Factors affecting heart rate include exercise, stress, caffeine, medications (beta-blockers), and medical conditions like thyroid disorders.

Normal oxygen saturation (SpO2) is 95-100% at sea level. Levels below 94% indicate potential hypoxemia and warrant medical evaluation. Below 90% is considered severe hypoxia requiring immediate attention. COPD patients may have acceptable levels of 88-92%. Factors affecting readings include altitude, smoking, lung diseases, anemia, and measurement errors. Common causes of low SpO2 include pneumonia, COPD exacerbation, pulmonary embolism, and heart failure.

High blood pressure (hypertension) management includes lifestyle modifications: reduce sodium intake to less than 2,300mg daily, maintain healthy weight (BMI 18.5-24.9), exercise regularly (150 minutes moderate aerobic activity weekly), limit alcohol, and quit smoking. First-line medications include ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril), ARBs (losartan, valsartan), CCBs (amlodipine), and diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide). Target blood pressure is less than 130/80 mmHg for most adults. Monitor at home and follow up with healthcare provider every 1-3 months.

Blood glucose targets for adults with diabetes: Fasting (before meals) 80-130 mg/dL, 1-2 hours after meals less than 180 mg/dL. HbA1c target less than 7% for most adults (indicates average blood sugar over 2-3 months). Symptoms of hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) include shakiness, sweating, confusion, and rapid heartbeat. Symptoms of hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) include increased thirst, frequent urination, and fatigue. Monitor glucose levels regularly, take medications as prescribed, and follow a balanced meal plan.

Normal respiratory rate for adults is 12-20 breaths per minute. Tachypnea (greater than 20 breaths/min) can indicate fever, anxiety, lung disease, heart failure, or metabolic disorders. Bradypnea (less than 12 breaths/min) may be caused by opioid use, brainstem injury, or hypothyroidism. Watch for signs of respiratory distress including use of accessory muscles, nasal flaring, retractions, and cyanosis. Pulse oximetry should be monitored alongside respiratory rate.

Normal body temperature ranges from 97-99°F (36.1-37.2°C). Fever is defined as 100.4°F (38°C) or higher. Hypothermia is below 95°F (35°C) and requires immediate medical attention. Factors affecting temperature include time of day (typically lowest in early morning), age, menstrual cycle, exercise, and infections. Common causes of fever include infections, inflammatory conditions, medications, and heat exposure. Antipyretics (acetaminophen, ibuprofen) can help reduce fever.

Chest pain requires urgent evaluation. Characteristics suggesting serious causes: crushing pressure, radiation to arm/jaw/neck, associated shortness of breath, nausea, sweating. Cardiac chest pain is often triggered by exertion and relieved by rest. Non-cardiac causes include GERD (burning, worse when lying down), musculoskeletal (sharp, worsens with movement), pleuritic (sharp, worse with breathing), and panic attacks (accompanied by anxiety and hyperventilation). Always seek immediate medical attention for new or unexplained chest pain.

Dyspnea (shortness of breath) causes include cardiac (heart failure, pulmonary embolism, arrhythmia), pulmonary (COPD, asthma, pneumonia, pneumothorax), metabolic (anemia, diabetic ketoacidosis), and psychological (anxiety, panic disorder). Acute dyspnea requires immediate evaluation. Chronic dyspnea should be evaluated with chest X-ray, ECG, pulmonary function tests, and labs. Oxygen therapy is indicated for SpO2 below 88%. Position patient upright if having difficulty breathing.

Common cardiovascular medications: Beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol) - slow heart rate and reduce blood pressure; ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) - vasodilation, may cause cough; Diuretics (furosemide, hydrochlorothiazide) - remove excess fluid; Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin) - lower cholesterol. Common diabetes medications: Metformin (first-line, improves insulin sensitivity), GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide) - weight loss and glucose control, Insulin (various types for meal coverage and basal needs). Always take medications as prescribed and report side effects to healthcare provider.

Signs and symptoms of heart failure: Shortness of breath (especially when lying flat - orthopnea), waking up breathless at night (PND), swelling in legs/anktes (edema), fatigue and weakness, rapid weight gain from fluid retention, decreased exercise tolerance, cough with frothy sputum. Left-sided failure causes pulmonary congestion; right-sided causes peripheral edema. EF (ejection fraction) measures pumping ability. Treatment includes diuretics, ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and lifestyle modifications (salt restriction, fluid limit, regular exercise).

Common heart rhythm disorders: Atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular, increases stroke risk - requires anticoagulation), Atrial flutter (sawtooth pattern), Supraventricular tachycardia (rapid heart rate from above AV node), Ventricular tachycardia (rapid rhythm from ventricles - medical emergency), Heart block (slowed electrical conduction). Diagnosis via ECG, Holter monitor, or event recorder. Treatment options include medications, cardioversion, catheter ablation, and pacemakers. CHA2DS2-VASc score guides anticoagulation in AF.

Pneumonia is an infection of the lungs causing inflammation and fluid in air sacs. Symptoms include cough (often productive), fever, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain, and fatigue. Types: Community-acquired (most common), Healthcare-associated, Ventilator-associated. Diagnosis via chest X-ray, CT scan, and labs. Treatment: Antibiotics (choice depends on type and severity), oxygen therapy, rest, hydration. Prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, flu), hand hygiene, quit smoking. Complications include pleural effusion, respiratory failure, and sepsis.

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Key symptoms: Chronic cough, sputum production, progressive shortness of breath. GOLD criteria classify severity (1-4) based on FEV1. Treatment: Bronchodilators (short-acting and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy for severe disease (SpO2 below 88%). Vaccinations: Flu annually, pneumococcal, COVID-19. Exacerbations require prompt treatment with steroids and antibiotics. Smoking cessation is essential.

Asthma is chronic airway inflammation causing wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. Triggers include allergens, exercise, cold air, infections, and stress. Classification: Intermittent, Mild persistent, Moderate persistent, Severe persistent. Treatment uses quick-relief inhalers (albuterol) for acute symptoms and controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, leukotriene modifiers, LABA) for long-term control. Action plan includes peak flow monitoring. Severe asthma may require biologic medications. Most can achieve good control with proper treatment.

Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by body's dysregulated response to infection. Warning signs (SOFA score components): Altered mental status, Respiratory rate 22 or greater, Systolic BP 100 mmHg or less, Heart rate greater than 90. Other signs: Fever above 101°F or below 96.8°F, confusion, decreased urine output, lactic acid greater than 2 mmol/L. Time is critical - hours matter. Treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, fluid resuscitation, vasopressors if needed. Follow Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines.

Stroke (CVA) occurs when blood supply to brain is interrupted. FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency. Types: Ischemic (87% - clot blocking artery), Hemorrhagic (13% - bleeding in brain). TIA (mini-stroke) is temporary and warns of impending stroke. Time-critical: Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) within 4.5 hours for ischemic stroke. Hospital treatment: CT/MRI, thrombectomy if large vessel occlusion, blood pressure management, rehabilitation. Prevention: Control BP, manage AF with anticoagulation, stop smoking.

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) is sudden kidney function decline. Causes: Pre-renal (low blood flow - dehydration, heart failure), Renal (intrinsic kidney damage - infection, toxins, glomerulonephritis), Post-renal (urinary obstruction). Diagnosis: Elevated creatinine, decreased urine output, BUN/creatinine ratio. Treatment: Address underlying cause, fluid management, avoid nephrotoxins, monitor electrolytes. Severe AKI may require dialysis. Prevention: Stay hydrated, avoid NSAIDs, monitor kidney function in high-risk patients.

Common electrolyte disorders: Hyponatremia (low sodium - less than 135 mEq/L) causes confusion, seizures; Hypernatremia (high sodium) causes thirst, neurological symptoms. Hypokalemia (low potassium less than 3.5) causes muscle weakness, arrhythmias; Hyperkalemia (high greater than 5.0) causes life-threatening arrhythmias. Hypocalcemia (low calcium) causes tetany, seizures; Hypercalcemia (high) causes confusion, kidney stones. Monitor levels in patients on diuretics, kidney disease, or heart failure.

Pain assessment uses scales: Numeric (0-10), Wong-Baker faces, FLACC for non-verbal. Types: Nociceptive (somatic, visceral), Neuropathic (burning, tingling). WHO analgesic ladder: Step 1 - Non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), Step 2 - Weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) for mild-moderate, Step 3 - Strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone) for severe. Adjuvants: Gabapentin for neuropathic, steroids for inflammatory. Non-pharmacological: Ice, heat, TENS, relaxation. Monitor for sedation and respiratory depression with opioids.

Wound healing phases: Inflammatory (0-3 days), Proliferative (3-21 days), Remodeling (21 days-1 year). Types: Acute (surgical, traumatic), Chronic (diabetic ulcers, pressure injuries). Assessment: Size, depth, drainage, odor, surrounding tissue. Treatment: Clean with saline, debride necrotic tissue, choose appropriate dressing (hydrocolloid, alginate, antimicrobial). Signs of infection: Increased pain, redness, warmth, pus, fever. Diabetic foot ulcers require offloading and meticulous care. Prevention: Proper nutrition, moisture management, pressure relief.

Common mental health disorders: Depression (PHQ-9 score: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe). Anxiety (GAD-7: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-21 severe). Suicide risk assessment: Ask about ideation, plan, intent, prior attempts, access to means. Warning signs: Withdrawal, mood changes, substance use, giving away possessions. Treatment: Psychotherapy (CBT, DBT), medications (SSRIs, SNRIs), crisis resources. National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 988.

Normal pediatric vital signs by age: Neonate (0-28 days): HR 100-160, RR 30-60, BP systolic 60-90. Infant (1-12 months): HR 100-160, RR 20-40, BP systolic 70-100. Toddler (1-3 years): HR 80-130, RR 20-30, BP systolic 80-110. Preschool (3-5 years): HR 80-120, RR 18-25, BP systolic 80-110. School age (6-12 years): HR 70-110, RR 14-22, BP systolic 90-120. Adolescent (12-18): HR 60-100, RR 12-20, BP systolic 90-120. Fever thresholds: Rectal temperature above 100.4°F (38°C) in infants under 3 months requires immediate evaluation.

Cancer staging: Stage I (localized), Stage II (local spread), Stage III (regional spread), Stage IV (metastatic). Common treatments: Surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, hormone therapy. Side effects: Fatigue, nausea, hair loss, immunosuppression, neuropathy. Common cancers: Breast (mammogram screening), Colorectal (colonoscopy starting age 45), Lung (low-dose CT for smokers), Cervical (Pap smear), Prostate (PSA testing). Early detection improves outcomes. Oncologist referral needed for diagnosis and treatment planning.

Common infections and treatments: URI (rest, fluids, decongestants - 7-10 days), Influenza (oseltamivir within 48 hours if high risk), Strep throat (penicillin or amoxicillin 10 days), UTI (nitrofurantoin 3-7 days), Pneumonia (antibiotics based on severity - 5-7 days), Cellulitis (antibiotics 7-14 days). Sepsis warning: Fever above 101°F, HR above 90, RR above 20, confusion, decreased urine output. Transmission prevention: Hand hygiene, isolation precautions, vaccination. MRSA: Contact precautions, wound care, antibiotics (clindamycin, TMP-SMX).

Daily nutritional requirements: Calories (adults 2000-2500 based on activity), Protein (0.8g/kg body weight), Fiber (25-35g), Fluids (8 cups/2L daily). Dietary guidelines: Balanced plate method - half vegetables/fruits, quarter protein, quarter whole grains. Sodium limit: Less than 2300mg daily (1500mg for hypertension). Added sugars: Less than 10% of calories. Special diets: DASH (hypertension), Mediterranean (cardiovascular health), diabetic (carbohydrate counting). Malnutrition signs: Unintentional weight loss, muscle wasting, edema, poor wound healing.

Elderly patients (65+) require modified care: Medications start low, go slow. Fall risk assessment (Timed Up and Go test - greater than 20 seconds increased risk). Cognitive screening (MMSE or MoCA). Polypharmacy review (5+ medications increases adverse events). Geriatric syndromes: Delirium, falls, incontinence, pressure ulcers, malnutrition. Vital signs: Orthostatic hypotension (drop in BP upon standing - check for dehydration, meds). Pain may be underreported - use nonverbal scales. Immunizations: Flu annually, pneumococcal, shingles (Shingrix), COVID-19. Advance care planning discussions.

Prenatal care: First visit 8-12 weeks, visits every 4 weeks until 28 weeks, then every 2 weeks until 36 weeks, then weekly. Key screenings: Blood type, Rh, CBC, HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, gestational diabetes (24-28 weeks),Group B strep (36 weeks). Warning signs: Vaginal bleeding, severe headache, vision changes, abdominal pain, decreased fetal movement, contractions before 37 weeks. Postpartum: Check-in at 1-2 weeks and 6 weeks. Postpartum depression: Screen using EPDS or PHQ-9. Baby blues resolve within 2 weeks; persistent symptoms require treatment.

Common musculoskeletal conditions: Osteoarthritis (joint pain, stiffness, treat with NSAIDs, physical therapy, joint replacement if severe). Rheumatoid arthritis (autoimmune, morning stiffness, treat with DMARDs). Fractures (immobilize, pain management, surgical fixation if needed). Back pain (conservative treatment first - activity modification, PT, NSAIDs; red flags: bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, trauma). Sprains/strains (RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation). Carpal tunnel (numbness/tingling, wrist splinting, surgery if severe). Hip fractures require urgent surgical intervention in elderly.

Common neurological conditions: Migraine (aura, photophobia, treat with triptans, prevent with beta-blockers or topiramate). Epilepsy (anticonvulsants based on seizure type, avoid triggers). Parkinson's (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia - treated with levodopa, dopamine agonists). Multiple sclerosis (MRI for diagnosis, disease-modifying therapies). Neuropathy (gabapentin, duloxetine for pain). Stroke (tPA within 4.5 hours, thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion). Headache red flags: Sudden severe thunderclap, fever, neck stiffness, confusion - suspect meningitis or hemorrhage. Seizure first aid: Turn on side, protect from injury, time the seizure, do not put anything in mouth.

GI conditions: GERD (heartburn, treat with PPIs, lifestyle modifications). Peptic ulcer disease (H. pylori eradication - amoxicillin + clarithromycin + PPI). IBD (Crohn's, ulcerative colitis - steroids, immunomodulators, biologics). Hepatitis (A/B/C screening, treatment varies by type). Cirrhosis (avoid alcohol, diuretics for ascites, screening for varices). Pancreatitis (alcohol gallstones - supportive care, NPO, IV fluids). Constipation (fiber, hydration, laxatives). Diarrhea (rehydration, loperamide if non-infectious). Colorectal cancer screening: Colonoscopy starting age 45, stool DNA test (Cologuard) every 3 years.

Thyroid conditions: Hypothyroidism (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation - treat with levothyroxine). Hyperthyroidism (weight loss, tremor, heat intolerance, palpitations - treat with methimazole, radioactive iodine, or surgery). Thyroid nodules (ultrasound evaluation, FNA if suspicious features). Hashimoto's (most common cause of hypothyroidism - autoimmune). TSH is the best screening test. Thyroid cancer warning signs: Hoarseness, difficulty swallowing, rapidly growing nodule. Pregnancy: TSH goals differ - consult endocrinologist. Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, wait 30-60 minutes before eating.

Allergic reactions: Mild (hives, itching, local swelling - antihistamine). Severe - Anaphylaxis (airway obstruction, hypotension, systemic symptoms). Anaphylaxis treatment: IM epinephrine 0.01mg/kg (max 0.3-0.5mg), repeat every 5-15 minutes if needed. Call 911. Position patient flat, elevate legs if hypotensive. Common allergens: Foods (nuts, shellfish, eggs, milk), Medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs), Insect stings, Latex. Allergic rhinitis: Intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, allergen avoidance. Asthma triggers: Allergens, exercise, cold air, infections. Skin testing identifies allergens. Immunotherapy for desensitization.

Emergency response: Cardiac arrest (CPR - 100-120 compressions/min, depth 2 inches, 30:2 ratio if intubated; defibrillate if VF/VT). Choking (Heimlich maneuver - abdominal thrusts above umbilicus). Seizure (protect airway, turn on side, do not restrain). Stroke (FAST - Face, Arm, Speech, Time - call 911 immediately). Anaphylaxis (epinephrine IM). Severe bleeding (direct pressure, elevate extremity, tourniquet if life-threatening). Shock (position flat, elevate legs unless spinal injury, keep warm). Heart attack (aspirin 325mg, nitroglycerin if BP above 90 systolic, oxygen if SpO2 below 94%). Always call 911 for emergencies.

Key lab normal ranges: CBC: WBC 4,500-11,000, Hgb 12-16 (F)/14-17 (M), Platelets 150,000-400,000. BMP: Na 135-145, K 3.5-5.0, Cl 96-106, CO2 23-29, BUN 7-20, Creatinine 0.6-1.2 (F)/0.7-1.3 (M), Glucose 70-100 fasting. LFTs: AST 10-40, ALT 7-56, ALP 44-147, TB 0.1-1.2. Lipids: Total cholesterol less than 200, LDL less than 100, HDL above 40 (F)/above 50 (M), Triglycerides less than 150. ABG: pH 7.35-7.45, PaO2 80-100, PaCO2 35-45, HCO3 22-26. Troponin less than 0.04 ng/mL. BNP less than 100 pg/mL.
